entry.title: What 20+ Years of Working with SAP Has Taught Me About Successful Transformations
entry.content:
What 20+ Years of Working with SAP Has Taught Me About Successful Transformations

Introduction

Over the past two decades, I have had the opportunity to work on SAP programs across multiple industries, including manufacturing, distribution, consumer products, and food services. During this journey, I have participated in SAP implementations, upgrades, global rollouts, business process transformations, and large-scale SAP S/4HANA migration programs.

Technology has changed significantly during this time. We have moved from traditional ERP implementations to intelligent enterprise platforms powered by real-time analytics, cloud technologies, automation, and artificial intelligence. However, despite these advancements, I have noticed that the factors that determine transformation success remain surprisingly consistent.

This article shares some of the most important lessons I have learned from working on SAP programs over the years.

Technology Alone Does Not Guarantee Success

Early in my career, I believed that selecting the right technology would automatically lead to project success. Over time, I learned that technology is only one part of the equation.

I have seen technically sound implementations struggle because business users were not prepared, process changes were not fully understood, or organizational alignment was missing.

The most successful SAP programs focus equally on people, processes, governance, and technology.

Organizations that treat SAP transformations as business transformations rather than IT projects typically achieve better outcomes.

Data Quality Matters More Than Most Organizations Expect

One recurring challenge I have observed across SAP programs is data quality.

Many organizations underestimate the effort required to cleanse, standardize, and validate data before migration activities begin.

In several transformation programs, data issues became one of the largest sources of testing delays, reconciliation challenges, and post-go-live support incidents.

My experience has taught me that data preparation should begin early and remain a continuous focus throughout the transformation lifecycle.

The quality of the migrated data often determines how quickly organizations realize value after deployment.

Governance Should Be Proactive, Not Reactive

Another lesson I have learned is that governance cannot be treated as a reporting exercise.

Many projects rely heavily on status updates and issue tracking. While these activities are important, effective governance requires early identification of risks and proactive decision-making.

The strongest transformation programs establish clear escalation paths, executive involvement, readiness monitoring, and structured decision-making processes from the beginning.

When governance becomes reactive, issues often grow larger before corrective actions are taken.

Business Engagement Is Critical

SAP transformations affect how people perform their daily work.

Because of this, business participation cannot be limited to requirements gathering and testing activities.

Successful programs consistently involve business stakeholders throughout the project lifecycle.

This includes:

  • Process design reviews

  • Data validation activities

  • Testing participation

  • Readiness assessments

  • Change management initiatives

The more engaged the business becomes, the smoother the transition tends to be after go-live.

Testing Is About Confidence, Not Just Defect Detection

Many organizations view testing primarily as a mechanism for identifying defects.

While defect identification is important, testing serves a broader purpose.

Testing builds confidence.

It helps project teams, business users, and leadership understand whether the organization is truly prepared for deployment.

When testing is approached as a business validation exercise rather than a technical checkpoint, the quality of outcomes often improves significantly.

The Importance of Executive Sponsorship

Throughout my career, I have observed a direct relationship between executive engagement and project success.

Executive sponsors help:

  • Remove organizational barriers

  • Resolve cross-functional conflicts

  • Secure resources

  • Accelerate decision-making

  • Reinforce transformation objectives

Programs with strong executive sponsorship typically maintain better momentum and experience fewer delays.

Go-Live Is Not the Finish Line

One of the biggest misconceptions in SAP programs is that success is measured by reaching go-live.

In reality, go-live represents the beginning of a new operational phase.

The true measure of success includes:

  • User adoption

  • Business process stability

  • Operational performance

  • Issue resolution effectiveness

  • Achievement of business objectives

Organizations that invest in hypercare, support readiness, and continuous improvement often realize greater long-term value from their SAP investments.

The Growing Role of AI in SAP Transformations

Recent advancements in artificial intelligence are creating new opportunities for SAP programs.

AI is increasingly being used to support:

  • Data quality monitoring

  • Predictive analytics

  • Risk identification

  • Testing optimization

  • Project governance

  • Operational insights

While AI is not a replacement for experience and judgment, it can significantly improve visibility and decision-making when applied appropriately.

I believe AI will continue to play an increasingly important role in future SAP transformation programs.

Final Thoughts

After more than 20 years of working with SAP, one lesson stands out above all others.

Successful transformations are rarely driven by technology alone.

They succeed when organizations align people, processes, data, governance, leadership, and technology toward a common objective.

Every SAP transformation presents unique challenges, but the principles of preparation, collaboration, business engagement, and continuous improvement remain constant.

As SAP technologies continue to evolve, these fundamentals will remain just as important as the platforms themselves.

From my experience, organizations that focus on these fundamentals consistently position themselves for stronger transformation outcomes and long-term business success.

entry.title: What We Learned Hosting a SAP Document AI Workshop
entry.content:
Introduction

Some weeks ago we had a hands-on event focused on SAP Document AI and enterprise document automation. We had planned it to be just a technical workshop, yet the attendees wasted no time in telling us all kinds of complications they were experiencing at the time. Soon, we were talking about operational processes, workflow complexity, and the increasing importance of AI in enterprise environments.

When we were planning the workshop we did not just want to show people a product. Our goal was to create a space where participants could engage directly with actual document processing situations, as well as investigate automation options that might enhance their routine workflows. I mean, you can't have an opinion about something you haven't used or experienced yourself.

During the session attendees had the opportunity to experiment with document classification, extraction models, workflow routing and automation processes related to larger SAP workflows.

As the Hackathon went on, the conversations naturally shifted toward some of the operational challenges many companies are currently facing. Although SAP Document AI was the focus, there was also room for repetitive manual work, fragmented validation processes, inconsistent document formats, and the difficulty of scaling document-heavy operations across teams and business units.

 

What the workshop was about

The workshop included product exploration and practical activities, simulating real enterprise scenarios. Rather than merely attend presentations or demos, participants were able to directly engage with workflows, classifiers, extraction schemas, and automation processes.

The event provided participants with insights into how SAP Document AI can support invoicing processes, free-form documents, workflow routing, and document classification.

 

One exercise that participants seemed to find particularly useful was the ROI calculator we brought to the workshop. While most of the day focused on workflows and automation scenarios, the calculator helped ground those discussions in something every organization cares about: business impact. It gave attendees a way to estimate how document automation could affect their own operations and helped move the conversation from technical possibilities to practical outcomes.

I think we were really taken aback by the diversity of perspectives in the room. People came from different industries and business environments, and brought very different operational realities and use cases to the discussion.

We were surprised that many of the issues being discussed were incredibly similar. Within all industries, companies had a lot of the same challenges around managing documents, approvals, integrations and keeping processes consistent. 

While working on practical document-processing scenarios, it became evident that the challenges most companies face go far beyond document extraction. As operations expand across multiple departments and teams, many organizations are still faced with fragmented workflows, repetitive manual validations, inconsistent document formats and processes that are becoming harder to manage.

And the workshop also reinforced something we often see when working with SAP technologies: real conversations happen once people can interact directly with a solution. The hackathon became much more practical and grounded after participants started to set up schemas, test classifiers and experiment with workflows. Rather than considering AI as something far away or theoretical, attendees began to consider how these solutions could realistically be integrated into their own operational environments.

Another important conclusion we arrived at was the importance of integration for successful AI adoption. Precise extraction models are crucial, no one will deny that, but they are just one part of the process. Companies also require workflows capable of adapting to operational changes, handling variations between document types, and collaborating effectively over time with existing systems.

Some Reflections

One of the most gratifying aspects of the workshop was to look back and see how quickly participants started to relate the exercises to their own organizations. Throughout the day the conversation evolved easily from examples under discussion to real business processes, internal priorities and potential next steps. Watching that transition in real time was a reminder of how the value of these sessions often goes far beyond the tech itself.

The workshop was also a great opportunity for us to hear directly from organizations that are facing different operational challenges, and to get a better understanding of how they are approaching document automation and AI adoption within their SAP landscapes.

Some of the most valuable insights came not only from the practical tasks but from the conversations that took place around them. The people who came had different perspectives, experiences and business contexts and we learnt from each other. The workshop was a valuable experience for all involved. As for me, I’m already looking forward to the next one, whether that’s as a host, a participant, or simply someone eager to keep learning.

entry.title: Purpose Built Intelligent Agents designed in SAP Joule Studio
entry.content:
AI agents have fundamentally transformed the way the IT industry operates. Beyond well‑recognized benefits—such as automation of complex tasks, increased productivity, scalability, continuous operation, and adaptive, intelligent decision‑making—the human‑in‑the‑loop approach ensures that authorized personnel remain informed, accountable, and actively involved in critical business decisions.

While numerous tools exist for building AI agents, a key challenge today is fragmentation. Agents developed using different tools and technologies often operate in silos, with limited data exchange and interoperability. This significantly restricts their overall effectiveness. Security further compounds this challenge, as agents built on diverse open‑source technologies may not consistently adhere to standardized agentic security and compliance frameworks. As a result, innovation can be constrained, and developers are required to continuously update and strengthen governance models.

SAP Joule Studio addresses these challenges by enabling organizations to deliver secure, scalable, and business‑centric agentic AI experiences tailored to their specific needs. As part of SAP Build, Joule Studio also leverages other SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) services. It integrates SAP AI Foundation for execution‑related components and SAP Integration Suite to govern and manage the APIs consumed by agents and skills. This cohesive architecture allows Joule Studio to stand out by delivering deeply integrated, enterprise‑grade skills and agents aligned with real business outcomes.

With SAP Joule Studio, organizations can design and deploy custom Joule skills and Joule agents tailored to specific business use cases, driving enhanced operational efficiency and innovation across the enterprise.

SAP has published couple of sample agents that can serve as an example in building your own business agents. These are free to use once you have obtained ‘SAP Build-default’ license plan.

Supplier Agent: Supplier Agent help users query supplier information using natural language, and the agent can aggregate data from diverse sources, enabling better and more optimized operational planning.
This sample agent uses 2 databases to retrieve required information.
1: SAP S/4HANA Cloud - Public Edition
2: Northwind Database: A third party free sample database containing data for a fictional food-import/export company, “Northwind Traders.” It Includes tables like Customers, Orders, Products, Employees, Suppliers, etc. We have used an OData API endpoint for customers and suppliers in this same agent. The supplier agent is designed in Joule studio capability of SAP Build and contains the below Joule Skills that are added as tools to the custom agent:
• Retrieve Customer Order Details
- Customer details are retrieved from the Northwind database via the OData API.
- Action project used: 'Northwind Actions'
• Retrieve Food Supplier Details (Northwind)
- Supplier details are retrieved from the Northwind database via the OData API.
- Action project used: 'Northwind Actions'
• Retrieve Technology Supplier Details S/4HANA
- Supplier details are retrieved from the SAP S/4HANA Cloud - Public Edition
- Action project used: 'Business Partner SAP S4HANA (A2X)'.

Procurement Risk Analyzer Agent: The Procurement Risk Analyzer agent helps procurement managers identify potential supplier delivery risks by analyzing open purchase orders and their expected delivery dates.
The agent retrieves open purchase orders from SAP S/4HANA Cloud and evaluates whether deliveries are overdue or at risk of delay. It can summarize late purchase orders, group them by supplier, and highlight suppliers that may cause delivery risks.
This agent is useful for questions about delayed deliveries, overdue purchase orders, supplier delivery reliability, and procurement risks.

Custom agents created by SAP Partners:

Several SAP partners have also developed AI agents, which are available as free content on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

1: Master Conversational Agent for Customer Order Processing  - by Infosys:

The Master Conversational Agent orchestrates multiple sub-agents to support sales order lifecycle management through natural language. It enables users to create, modify, track, and resolve sales orders efficiently while enforcing business rules and data consistency. Future SAP BTP integration enables centralized governance, monitoring, and continuous improvement.

2: Material Management and Sales Operations Agent  - by Blueprint Technologies:

The process supports end-to-end material inquiry and sales execution through an intelligent agent. When a user requests material availability, the agent retrieves real-time stock information by invoking the Material Stocks service and presents current quantity and status. If the user asks for alternatives, the agent triggers the Material Determination capability to identify and return valid substitute materials based on system configuration. When the user proceeds to create a sales order, the agent collects any missing mandatory inputs, invokes the Sales Order service to create the order, and confirms successful creation with order details. 

The ‘Material Management and Sales Operations agent’ is designed in Joule studio capability of SAP Build and contains the below Joule Skills that are added as tools to the custom agent: 

  • Material Stocks - Retrieves Materials details for a specified Material from SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud 
  • Material Substitution - A skill to retrieve material substitution for products from SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud 
  • Sales Order - A skill to create sales based on customer. /Product details in SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud

3: SAP EHS Incident Agent  - by Wipro:

The EHS Incident Agent is responsible for creating, categorizing, and displaying safety incidents across plant locations in compliance with established rules. Incidents are classified under three categories: 001 - Incident, 002 - Near Miss and 003 - Safety Observation. Each incident must reference an active EHS Location ID, include a title, and capture key details such as date, time, and description.

Core Value Proposition: Our agent empowers employees to capture first-hand information at the exact moment an incident occurs by enabling the person on the scene to update the system immediately, we eliminate 'memory fade' and ensure the most accurate safety data is recorded directly into SAP.

  • Agent is Integrated with SAP S/4HANA API 
    • API_EHS_REPORT_INCIDENT_SRV
  • Agent uses Joule Skills for retrieving and validating the EHS Incident Location.
    • EHSM_IM_GET: Retrieving EHS Incidents List
    • EHSM Location ID details: EHS Location Details
    • EHSM_IM: Creating EHS Incident for Incident categories 001(Incident), 002(Near Miss) & 003(Safety Observation).

More Partner Agents can be discovered in SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

 SAP Build Solutions in SAP Store: SAP partners have designed and built agents and SAP Build Solutions that are available on the SAP Store and can be downloaded upon payment of the listed price for each offering. One of the most popular Agentic solutions in SAP store built by partner is:

Smarter procurement decisions with pegged requirement analysis – by Motiveminds

SAP Build , Joule Studio , Joule , Agentic AI 

@Archana, @nayak_aj

entry.title: A Practical Approach to Automating External Parameter Changes in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

As SAP Integration Suite developers, we regularly need to adjust external parameter values throughout a project’s lifecycle. This is more than a good practice: it helps ensure that interfaces remain tenant-agnostic and easier to maintain across environments.

In many projects, these adjustments are only required during transport between environments. In more complex landscapes, however, what appears to be a simple task can quickly become a major operational challenge for any CPI specialist.

 

Example Scenario

Imagine you have built multiple interfaces and your project also includes SAP standard packages such as Cell and Gene Therapy Orchestration (CGTO) or SAP Digital Manufacturing (DM). In addition, SAP Event Mesh is being used as the event broker for CGTO.

Although this setup may appear straightforward, complications often arise at the architectural level. For reasons such as budget constraints or time pressure, the DEV and QA landscapes in this example include not only the standard systems but also hotfix systems dedicated to specific testing activities.

 

 

 

In the DEV scenario described above, SAP Integration Suite acts as the central backbone for all communication flows. While there may be two instances of SAP CGTO, two of SAP S/4HANA, and so on, there is only one SAP Integration Suite tenant responsible for connecting these systems correctly.

Once this landscape is defined, SAP Integration Suite must be adjusted accordingly to ensure that all communication channels function correctly. This becomes especially critical when the hotfix testing window is extremely limited and the parallel system remains offline. These adjustments can involve hundreds or even thousands of configuration values, making it unrealistic to update them manually within such a short period without a significant risk of error.

To illustrate the scale of the challenge, nearly 200 external parameters may be affected in a scenario like this, for example, SAP clients, queue paths, and system endpoints.

 

Solution Overview

A practical way to address this challenge is to leverage SAP Integration Suite itself, more specifically, the integration content available through SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

This approach makes it possible to extract the affected external parameters, maintain them in a spreadsheet, save them as a CSV file, and submit the update through an external tool such as Postman. The effort of preparing the configuration files is done only once; after that, switching between environment-specific configurations can be completed in just a few minutes by uploading the appropriate file.

 

Soft Configurations Framework

The concept is straightforward: this interface provides a controlled way to manage external parameters in SAP Integration Suite through an external client such as Postman.

 The goal is to reduce manual configuration effort across multiple environments while improving traceability and lowering the risk of human error.

 

How the Solution Works

Below are the iFlows included in this solution scope.

Get iFlows

This iFlow retrieves the external parameters of iFlows within the selected SAP Integration Suite packages and returns the result in either JSON or CSV format.

Options:

  • ignorePackages

Because of the large number of packages involved, this option was created to exclude any packages that fall outside the scope of the hotfix, for example, sandbox packages used for testing or other interfaces that are completely unrelated to the hotfix process.

  • returnPackage

If you only need to query a specific package, this option is much simpler, and significantly faster, than downloading every package available on the tenant.

  • Csv

By default, the interface returns the results in JSON format, which is convenient for inspection and integration. For SAP standard packages or large-scale parameter updates, however, CSV is often the most practical option because it allows all parameters to be reviewed and maintained efficiently in Microsoft Excel.

  • csvDelimiter

The user can also select the delimiter to be used in the CSV output.

 

Update Soft Config

This iFlow reads a JSON or CSV payload and updates the corresponding iFlows within the defined scope. It returns a full JSON report in Postman showing the affected external parameters, the previous and updated values, and the response status for each change.

Options:

  • Email

Because a full JSON report of the affected external parameters is generated, the interface can also send this report to a designated email inbox. This is particularly useful for traceability and audit purposes.

  • Deploy

This option allows the affected iflows to have their changes saved or saved and deployed.

 

iFlow Design and Processing Logic

 

Get All iFlows

Bellow are all the steps for this interface development.

Integration Process

  • Headers – Content Modifier: Reads all header parameters sent by a third-party application, such as Postman.
  • Read CSV Header – Groovy Script: Checks whether a CSV file has been requested.
  • Get Packages – Process Call: Invokes the local Get Packages process.

Get Packages

  • Get Packages – Request Reply: Retrieves all packages from SAP Integration Suite for the specified tenant (defined directly in the URL).
  • Read Packages – Groovy Script: Processes the returned payload and performs the following actions:
    • If returnPackage is enabled, filters the payload based on the packages specified in the client request.
    • If ignorePackages is enabled, returns all packages except those specified in the client request.
    • Creates a clean internal JSON structure containing the package ID, Name, and ShortText.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

def slim = results.collect { r ->
        [
            Id        : r?.Id ?: "",
            Name      : r?.Name ?: "",
            ShortText : r?.ShortText ?: ""
        ]
}
  • Total Packages – Router: If no packages are returned, the process terminates.
  • Get Integrations – Looping Process Call: Invokes the Get Artifacts from Package process for each package.

 

Get Artifacts from Package

  • Header – Content Modifier: Resets the current iFlow count.
  • Get Actual Package – Groovy Script: Since the next step requires the package ID for the request, this script retrieves the current package ID from the internal JSON message.
  • Get Artifacts from Package – Request Reply: Retrieves all iFlows associated with the selected package ID.
  • Get iFlows – Groovy Script: Processes the returned payload and appends the iFlow ID, Name, and ShortText, along with the current package ID, to the internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

found = false

jsonAll.each { item ->

	if ((item instanceof Map) && String.valueOf(item.Id) == targetPackage) 
	{
		item.Iflows = slim
		found = true
	}
}
  • Empty? – Router: If no iFlows are returned, the process ends and proceeds to the next package.
  • Get Soft Configurations – Looping Process Call: Invokes the local Get Soft Configurations process.

 

Get Soft configurations

  • Get iFlow – Groovy Script: For the package currently in scope, retrieves each iFlow ID required to request its soft configurations in the next step.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if(resultValue)
{      
	message.setProperty("iflowID", resultValue)
	message.setProperty("currentCountIflow", targetIflowIx + 1)
}
  • Get iFlow Soft Configs – Request Reply: Retrieves all soft configurations for each iFlow.
  • Get Soft Configs and Update – Groovy Script: Processes the retrieved information by cleaning the message, filtering out soft configurations defined directly within the iFlow, and appending the remaining entries to the internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    def body       = message.getBody(String) ?: '{}'
    def parsed     = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
    def results    = parsed?.d?.results ?: []

    def slim = results.collect {
        [
            Parameter   : it?.ParameterKey   ?: "",
            Value       : it?.ParameterValue ?: ""
            //Description : it?.Description    ?: ""
        ]
    }

    ...

    def pkg = jsonAll.find { it?.Id == packageID }

    // Ignore list
    def ignoreList = ignore.split(",")
                        .collect { it.trim() }
                        .findAll { it }

    // remove from list
    slim = slim.findAll { r -> !(r?.Parameter in ignoreList) }    

    if (pkg?.Iflows) {
        // Find the iflow
        def flow = pkg.Iflows.find { it?.IdIflow == iflowID }
        if (flow) {
            flow.SoftConfig = slim
        }
    }
...
}

 

  Integration Process

  • Return Message – Content Modifier: Loads the internal JSON message into the message body.
  • isCSV? – Router: Checks whether a CSV output was requested. If not, the process ends and returns the internal JSON message.
  • CSV Convert – Groovy Script: Converts the JSON message into CSV format using the specified delimiter.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

// HEADER
    StringBuilder csv = new StringBuilder()
    csv.append(["PackageID","PackageName","IdIflow","NameIflow","Parameter","Value"].join(separator))
       .append(nl)

    // Clear escapes
    def esc = { v ->
        if (v == null) return ""
        def s = v.toString()
        def needsQuotes = s.contains(separator) || s.contains('"') || s.contains('\n') || s.contains('\r')
        s = s.replace('"', '""')
        needsQuotes ? "\"${s}\"" : s
    }

    // packages
    json.each { pkg ->
        def pkgId   = pkg.Id
        def pkgName = pkg.Name
        def iflows  = pkg.Iflows ?: []

        // iFlows
        iflows.each { iflow ->
            def iflowId   = iflow.IdIflow
            def iflowName = iflow.NameIflow
            def softCfgs  = iflow.SoftConfig ?: []

            // SoftConfig
            softCfgs.each { cfg ->
                def param = cfg.Parameter
                def value = cfg.Value

                csv.append([
                    esc(pkgId),
                    esc(pkgName),
                    esc(iflowId),
                    esc(iflowName),
                    esc(param),
                    esc(value)
                ].join(separator)).append(nl)
            }
        }
    }
  • Header – Content Modifier: Updates the Content-Type header to text/csv.
  • End: Terminates the process.

 

Update Soft Configurations

Bellow are all the steps for this interface development.

Integration Process

  • Count – Content Modifier: Reads all configurations and header parameters sent by a third-party application, such as Postman.
  • Validate JSON Message – Groovy Script: Checks whether the incoming message is in JSON or CSV format, validates its structure, and transforms the payload into an internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    ...
        
    // is JSON?
    try {
        parsed = slurper.parseText(body)
        if (parsed instanceof Map || parsed instanceof List) {
            isJson = true
        }
    } catch (Exception e) {
        isJson = false
    }

    // if its json, end
    if (!isJson) 
    {
        ...
    }
    else
    {
        /////////////////////////////////////////
        //json
        /////////////////////////////////////////
        
        try 
        {
            json = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
        } catch (e) {
            error = "Invalid JSON format."
        }

        // array?
        if (!(json instanceof List)) {
            error = "JSON must be an array at root level."
        }

        json.eachWithIndex { item, idx ->

            // IdIflow
            if (!item?.IdIflow || !(item.IdIflow instanceof String)) {
                error = "Element at index ${idx} missing valid 'IdIflow' (string)."
            }

            // SoftConfig
            if (!(item?.SoftConfig instanceof List)) {
                error = "Element '${item.IdIflow}' missing valid 'SoftConfig' array."
            }

            // config
            item.SoftConfig.eachWithIndex { cfg, cidx ->
                if (!cfg?.Parameter || !(cfg.Parameter instanceof String)) {
                    error = "Invalid 'Parameter' in '${item.IdIflow}' at SoftConfig index ${cidx}."
                }
                if (cfg.Value == null) { // allowed empty, but not null
                    error = "Missing 'Value' in '${item.IdIflow}' at SoftConfig index ${cidx}."
                }
            }
        }
    }
    def deploy = properties.get("deploy")
        
    if(!deploy)
    {
        message.setProperty("deploy", 'NO')
    }
    else
    {
        if( deploy.toUpperCase() != 'YES')
            message.setProperty("deploy", 'NO')
        else
            message.setProperty("deploy", 'YES')        
    }
...
}

 

  • All Good? – Router: Verifies whether the message is valid. If validation fails, the process ends and returns an error message.
  • iFlow – Looping Process Call: Invokes the local iFlow Logic process.

 

Iflow Logic

  • Get iFlow – Groovy Script: Reads information for each iFlow from the internal payload.
  • Get SoftConfigs – Request Reply: Retrieves the existing soft configurations for the iFlow currently in scope.
  • Store Previous Configs – Content Modifier: Stores the response payload for later use.
  • Fetch Token – Process Call: Invokes the Fetch Token local process.

 

Fetch Token

  • Header – Content Modifier: Configures the request to fetch the X-CSRF token header required for the next call.
  • Fetch Token – Request Reply: Retrieves the token through a GET request.
  • End: Terminates the local process and returns control to the iFlow Logic process call. 

 

 Iflow Logic

  • Soft Configs – Looping Process Call: Iteratively invokes the local Soft Configs process for each configuration item.

 

Soft Configs

  • Get Soft Config – Groovy Script: Processes each soft parameter individually for the iFlow currently in scope.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if (configIndex < 0 || configIndex >= obj.SoftConfig.size()) 
{
   message.setProperty("currentParameter", "")
   message.setProperty("currentParameterValue", "")
}
else
{
   def value     = obj.SoftConfig[configIndex]?.Value?.toString()
   def parameter = obj.SoftConfig[configIndex]?.Parameter?.toString()

   message.setProperty("currentParameterValue", value)
   message.setProperty("currentParameter", parameter)
}
  • Prepare Body – Content Modifier: Configures the request body in the expected format for the next call and sets the required CSRF token headers.
  • Update Config – Request Reply: Updates the current soft parameter through a PUT request.
  • Read Response – Groovy Script: Reads the HTTP response code and message, while also retrieving the previous value that was cached earlier in the process.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    def body         = message.getBody(String)
    def jsonPrevious = message.getProperty("jsonPrevious")?.toString()
    def parameterKey = message.getProperty("currentParameter")?.toString()
    def head         = message.getHeaders();
    def httpCode     = head.get("CamelHttpResponseCode");

    def json         = new JsonSlurper().parseText(jsonPrevious)

    def results      = json?.d?.results
    def node         = results.find { it?.ParameterKey == parameterKey }
    def value        = node?.ParameterValue?.toString()

    message.setProperty("beforeParameterValue", value)
    message.setProperty("responseMessage", body)
    message.setProperty("responseCode", httpCode)

    return message
}
  • JSON Final Message – Groovy Script: Updates the internal JSON message with the new values obtained from the previous step.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

def newCfg = [
        Parameter      : parameter,
        ValueBefore    : valueBefore,
        ValueUpdated   : valueUpdated,
        ResponseCode   : responseCode,
        ResponseMessage: responseMsg
]

def existingIdx = node.SoftConfig.findIndexOf { it?.Parameter == parameter }

if (existingIdx >= 0) 
{
   node.SoftConfig[existingIdx] = newCfg
} 
else 
{
   node.SoftConfig << newCfg
}
  • End: Once the loop finishes, control returns to the iFlow Logic process and execution continues.

 

iFlow Logic

  • Deploy? – Router: Checks whether deployment was requested by the user.
  • Deploy – Request Reply: Sends a POST request using the iFlow ID currently in scope to trigger deployment.
  • Update JSON – Groovy Script: Updates the internal JSON message with the deployment result.
  • End: If no additional iFlows remain, control returns to the Integration Process.

 

Integration Process

  • Final Payload – Content Modifier: Loads the internal JSON message into the message body.
  • Monitor – Groovy Script: Attaches the payload to the MPL.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if (messageLog != null) 
{
   messageLog.addAttachmentAsString('Soft config report', body, 'text/plain')
}
  • Has Email – Router: Checks whether an email request was provided. If not, the process ends.
  • Send Email – Send: Sends an email with the payload attached.

 

Iflow configuration

Get all iflows

Receiver

ParameterValue
ReceiverGet_Soft_Configurations
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ]/api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts(Id='${property.iflowID}',Version='active')/Configurations
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodGET
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverGet_Iflows
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationPackages('${property.packageID}')/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodGet
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverGet_Packages
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationPackages
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodGet
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept

More

ParameterValue
Change1Test 1
Change 2Test 2
Change 3Test 3
IgnoreParameterSAP_ProfileID

Change1-3: Dummy external parameters for testing. Have no effect on the iflow. 

IgnoreParameter: Add parameters that should not be returned on the response.

 

Change Soft Configs

Receiver

ParameterValue
ReceiverDeploy
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/DeployIntegrationDesigntimeArtifact?Id='${property.IdIflowExtracted}'&Version='active'
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodPOST
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept, X-CSRF-Token, Cookie

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverGetToken
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts(Id='Get_all_iflows',Version='active')/Configurations
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodGET
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept, X-CSRF-Token

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverUpdateSoftConfigs
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts(Id='${property.IdIflowExtracted}',Version='active')/$links/Configurations('${property.currentParameter}')
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodPUT
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name
[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept, X-CSRF-Token, Cookie

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverGetSoftConfigs
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts(Id='${property.IdIflowExtracted}',Version='active')/Configurations
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodGET
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverEmailNotification
Adapter TypeMail
Address[email address]
Proxy TypeInternet
Protection
[email protection]
Authentication[email auth]
Credential Name[email credential]
From
[email from]
To${property.userEmail}
SubjectIntegration Suite | Soft configurations change | ${property.tenant}

More

ParameterValue
Tenant[cpi tenant]

Tenant: used for the email subject only.

 

Example of a call through Postman

In this case, I’m returning the SoftConfigurations package.

JSON file format

CSV, with ; as delimiter

 

Now, let us move forward with the following change:

[
    {
        "IdIflow": "Get_all_iflows",
        "SoftConfig": [
            {
                "Parameter": "change1",
                "Value": "It works from Postman 1"
            },
            {
                "Parameter": "change2",
                "Value": "It works from Postman 2"
            }
        ]
    }
]

Call Change Soft Configs using the payload above, requesting both deployment and email notification.

Email report

And the attachment

[
    {
        "IdIflow": "Get_all_iflows",
        "Deploy": {
            "Required": "YES",
            "ResponseCode": 202,
            "ID": "---"
        },
        "SoftConfig": [
            {
                "Parameter": "change1",
                "ValueBefore": "Test 1",
                "ValueUpdated": "It works from Postman 1",
                "ResponseCode": "202",
                "ResponseMessage": ""
            },
            {
                "Parameter": "change2",
                "ValueBefore": "Test 2",
                "ValueUpdated": "It works from Postman 2",
                "ResponseCode": "202",
                "ResponseMessage": ""
            }
        ]
    }
]

If I go to the iflow configuration, I can see that the update was done.

Applicability

 

Interface Connection Management

This was the primary motivation for creating the interface. Even with strong controls in place, manual configuration always carries some risk. Storing configurations in separate files not only saves time, but also provides a more reliable and resilient operating model. Teams can maintain environment-specific configurations independently and apply them when needed.

 

Technical Documentation

Documenting external parameters and their values is rarely a pleasant task. With this interface, parameters can be exported as a CSV file, refined in Microsoft Excel, and then reused in technical documentation with far less manual effort.

 

Configurations After Transport to Another Environment

From a governance perspective, managing critical parameters in a controlled format can be preferable to maintaining them directly in a web interface. CSV files reduce the risk of mistakes, especially in scenarios where SAP CTMS transports interfaces with incorrect external parameters.

They also make it easier to identify patterns and provide a broader, more structured view of the configuration landscape.

 

Final thoughts

I created this tool to address a highly time-consuming challenge in day-to-day delivery work. As a result, it was designed with enough flexibility to evolve and support additional use cases over time. Potential enhancements could include AI-based support for technical documentation or simplified access for non-technical colleagues and clients who need a clearer view of active integrations.

Unfortunately, time, or more precisely, the lack of it, has been the main constraint preventing further development of these additional capabilities.

I hope you find this tool as useful as I found the process of creating it. There are, of course, many ways to solve the same problem; this is simply one approach that I enjoyed designing and implementing.

 

Groovy scripts

All groovy scripts found on this article can be downloaded from the following GitHub page.

Sap CPI softConfigs

entry.title: Guiding your customer through the new API policy and ODP-RFC
entry.content:
The way customers connect to SAP systems is changing, and you can feel it in almost every integration discussion.

This session, “Guiding your customer through the new API policy and ODP-RFC”, helps you make sense of that shift and turn it into something practical for your day-to-day work.

SAP is refining how data is accessed to improve security and consistency, especially as AI and automation become part of more solutions. Plus, topics like ODP-RFC scope and non-SAP access are getting clearer boundaries.

Honestly, the thing is, many customers will not notice the policy changes first. They will notice integration behavior, rate limits, or new constraints. That is where your guidance matters.

We will cover governed data access, SAP Business Data Cloud for non-SAP scenarios, and the April 26, 2026 API policy. You will also see how Agent2Agent fits into modern integration patterns and how to plan migrations step by step.

If you are in consulting, sales, or architecture, this session gives you a clear way to support customer conversations with confidence.

Register now and join the discussion. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/may/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014315.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPIntegration #DataGovernance

entry.title: Calling All Applicants for the 2026 Global Delivery Hub Americas High School Summer Orientation!
entry.content:
Each year, SAP’s Global Delivery Hub Americas (formerly Premium Hub CoE NA) hosts high school students to participate in a weeklong Summer Orientation. The 25-hour program provides students with the opportunity to learn about SAP technologies through different activities while receiving educational and career guidance from professionals in the field. SAP experts lead daily, interactive sessions that cover a variety of exciting topics, from coding and data analytics to SAP Cloud, IoT, and Mobile platforms. Over the course of the week, students use their technical skills and newly acquired knowledge to resolve real-world scenarios using SAP technologies. This year we are excited to host the orientation in-person at the Newtown Square campus (outside of Philadelphia). There, students will be able to explore the office, as well as participate in hands-on activities.   

Every year the Summer Orientation features a jam-packed schedule, from expert innovation sessions to career development guidance. The week starts off with a brief introduction of SAP along with a presentation on the various internship opportunities offered to college students. Students also have a career tip session, where they receive professional advice about their educational journey and beyond. Later in the week, students get to meet Balaji Gaddam, SVP Head of Global Delivery Hub Americas. 

Students work on a primary project throughout the week that is always relevant to the latest in technology. The students’ work has evolved over the years, much like how SAP’s products have evolved as well! In previous years of the program, students have built projects using a wide variety of SAP technologies. At the end of the week, students will be able to showcase their project to their fellow peers and Delivery Hub Team members.

High school students interested in attending the 2026 Summer Orientation should fill out the form below and update their most recent transcript. Space is limited for this competitive program and is geared towards rising Juniors and Seniors. Transcripts are required to be considered for the program so they must be uploaded to be considered. No resumes or cover letters are needed. Ideal candidates will have a strong interest in STEM and a desire to pursue further study and/or a career in a STEM-related field.  This is NOT an internship. 

Apply to the STEM Week here with your transcript


Applications will be accepted until Friday, June 12. Students accepted to the program will be notified of the decision by Friday, June 26. The orientation will run in-person at the SAP Newtown Square Campus from July 27 to July 31.  The general hours are 10 am to 3 pm but early dismissal on Friday. Transportation will need to be arranged for the student as we do not provide this. Please note that this is not an internship, it is an unpaid program, and all costs associated with travel will need to be covered by the student. SAP will not cover any costs.

entry.title: What a Document AI Workshop Taught Me About Clean Core
entry.content:
There is something I enjoy almost as much as writing content about SAP, and that is reading what people have to say in popular discussion forums regarding topics that may be seen as rather controversial. I think that many times, the fact that some themes generate so much discussion has more to do with people not really understanding them than with the proper complexity of the product, strategy or idea.

In the last couple of weeks, I read a lot of discussion around Clean Core, both in SAP Community and in these online forums. I was amazed by how people defined the concept. I mean, you could tell that the opinion they were communicating was, of course, directly linked to the way they each define Clean Core, and that is exactly what surprised me: everyone defined it differently. Some described Clean Core as a way to reduce customization, others associated it with cloud ERP, and a few questioned whether it was even realistic in large SAP environments. 

This reading coincided with the preparation and hosting of our hands-on SAP Document AI workshop together with SAP, although at first I didn’t see any connection between the discussions about Clean Core and the hackathon, by the end of the workshop, I found myself thinking about Clean Core in a way I hadn't before. 

In this case, our workshop was focused on a particular scenario involving document processing. Prior to the event, we had already created a Proof of Concept for one of our customers, who was working with a huge number of documents related to their operations from various regions and business units. The problem lay in the fact that it was not just about data extraction from invoices and tickets. Rather, the issue here was the high percentage of manual labor required for proper document classification, validation, and linking every file to the right business process. During our preparation for the presentation, we paid most of our attention to AI possibilities provided by SAP Document AI.

The unexpected aspect of the workshop discussions turned out to be the shift of focus to an area completely unrelated to what was being discussed in the first place. Naturally, people did show interest in the accuracy of document extraction, and in capabilities of AI as well. However, most of the questions were related to integration and scalability, how the solution could work in participants' environments, how well it would integrate with other applications, and how it would help them expand their capabilities without disrupting existing processes. 

Looking back, this is probably where the connection with Clean Core started to become clearer for me. Throughout the workshop, nobody was asking how to modify SAP so that Document AI could work. Nobody was discussing new custom tables, custom transactions, or large changes to core ERP processes. Several participants were already thinking about additional use cases, while others wanted to understand how the approach could scale across different document types and business units. The most prominent questions were adoption, integration, operational impact, and long-term scalability. The conversations focused on how to introduce a new capability while keeping the existing landscape stable.

That realization made me think about many of the online discussions I had been reading. A large number of them seemed to describe Clean Core primarily in terms of restrictions. The conversation often revolved around what organizations should avoid doing, what should not be customized, or what should be moved outside the ERP. While those aspects are certainly part of the concept, the workshop made me wonder if that perspective is too narrow.

What I saw during the event was not a limitation on innovation. If anything, it was the opposite. SAP Document AI was introducing completely new capabilities into existing business processes. The innovation was real, the business value was clear, and yet none of it required redesigning the ERP core itself. For the first time, I started thinking about Clean Core not as a strategy for reducing customization, but as a strategy for deciding where innovation should happen.

The more I thought about it after the workshop, the more I understood why so many people define Clean Core differently. Some people approach it from an architecture perspective, others from governance, and others from the realities of cloud ERP. Not one of these definitions can be wrong per se. But I found myself asking whether it is because of the lack of time spent on analyzing what the tool actually does for us and allows us to do.

In retrospect, what really got through to me at the workshop was not the demo as such. We had worked on getting ready the Proof of Concept, exercises and scenarios that would be demonstrated at the workshop during weeks in advance. What stayed with me after the workshop were the discussions triggered by all these. The most engaging ones did not concern the changes in ERP but rather organization of the processes that allowed introducing new functionality and retaining flexibility in the process. As much as I am not sure yet that there is such a definition of Clean Core accepted by all, after reading multiple threads online I am only even surer about this.

For me, Clean Core is no longer just about keeping systems standard. It is about creating the conditions for innovation to happen without making future change more difficult than it needs to be. Perhaps that is why I now see Clean Core less as a technical principle and more as a question every organization eventually has to answer: where should new capabilities live?

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #3: Bridging OT and IT: Delivering Mission-Critical Grid Data to SAP BTP Safely, S
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #3: Bridging OT and IT: Delivering Mission-Critical Grid Data to SAP BTP Safely, Scalably, and Cleanly

1.      Introduction

Utilities are accelerating their digital transformation, but one architectural frontier remains notoriously difficult to conquer:

How do you safely ingest high-frequency Operational Technology (OT) data from substations, feeders, and Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) into SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) without introducing cyber vulnerabilities or disrupting grid reliability?

Legacy architecture often relied on brittle, custom-coded point-to-point connections or heavy middleware that violated basic security isolation principles. This article provides a comprehensive, production-ready, architecture-driven blueprint for securely integrating:

  • IEC 61850 substation telemetry (GOOSE/MMS)
  • MQTT/Sparkplug B telemetry streams
  • SCADA / RTU / IED asynchronous events
  • Grid-edge IoT sensors

We will achieve this by leverage modern event-driven architectures (EDA), Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA), and SAP Clean Core design patterns.

2.      Why OT-to-IT Integration Challenges Enterprise

Bridging the physical grid and the digital cloud requires reconciling two fundamentally opposing engineering philosophies:

Dimension

Operational Technology (OT)

Enterprise Information Technology (IT)

Primary Priority

Availability & Safety: Human life and grid stability. Zero downtime tolerated.

Confidentiality & Integrity: Data security, compliance, and financial accuracy.

Protocols

Industrial, low-overhead, non-IP, or deterministic (IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus).

Web-native, human-readable, RESTful (HTTPS, OData, gRPC).

Network Philosophy

Air-gapped, strictly segmented (Purdue Model Level 0-3), highly localized.

Cloud-native, perimeterless, distributed, global access.

Data Characteristics

Ultra-high frequency (milliseconds), time-series bursts, massive velocity.

Transactional, structured, stateful, request-response.

To bridge this gap safely, cloud ingestion must be completely outbound-only from the lower levels, strictly audited, cryptographically verified, and decoupled via an enterprise event broker.

3.      Business impact on Utilities

This architecture is not just a technical modernization—it directly enables:

  • Reduced unplanned outages through real-time anomaly detection
  • Lower maintenance costs via predictive asset health insights
  • Improved regulatory compliance with secure audit-ready telemetry flows
  • Faster field response through automated S/4HANA work order generation
  • Scalable grid-edge onboarding without re-platforming legacy OT systems

4.      Reference Architecture: Purdue Model to SAP BTP

The architecture below maps the telemetry flow across the classic Purdue Model for Industrial Control Systems, ensuring that no direct connection is ever established between the cloud and Level 2/3 critical infrastructure.

5.      Architectural Safeguards Highlighted:

  • Directional Isolation: Firewalls block all inbound traffic to the Substation OT network. The Industrial Edge Gateway establishes an outbound connection to the Upper DMZ broker.
  • Decoupling Layer: SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) acts as the shock absorber, ensuring that high-frequency grid telemetry bursts do not overwhelm downstream ERP APIs.

*Architectural Principle: Direct OT-to-cloud integrations without DMZ brokers are inherently unsafe.  Any architecture that bypasses the industrial DMZ violates fundamental Purdue Model isolation and must be avoided.

6.      Deep-Dive: Protocol Translation & Topic Design

 The Conversion: IEC 61850 to Lightweight Cloud Events: IEC 61850 uses Manufacturing Message Specification (MMS) for complex reporting and Generic Object-Oriented Substation Events (GOOSE) for peer-to-peer microsecond trips. These cannot be parsed by cloud apps directly.

An Edge Gateway maps these complex hierarchical Logical Nodes (e.g., MMXU1 for Three-Phase Voltage/Current) into standard JSON structures wrapped in the CloudEvents specification.

Production-Grade Ingestion Payload Example

Below is the exact schema passed from the Upper DMZ to SAP Advanced Event Mesh when a transformer experiences a thermal anomaly:

JSON { "specversion": "1.0",

  "id": "evt-773a-4b92-88ef-22114949ad45", "source": "/us-east/substation-s12/transformer-tx03",   "type":

"com.utility.grid.telemetry.v1",

  "datacontenttype": "application/json",

  "time": "2026-06-01T18:21:55.003Z",

  "data": { "logical_node": "YPTR1",

    "component": "WindingTemperature",

    "metrics": {  "temperature_celsius": 98.4,

      "ambient_celsius": 32.1,

      "cooling_fan_status": "ON_MAX" },

    "status": { "health_score": 72.5,

      "anomaly_detected": true,

      "severity": "WARNING" } } }

Enterprise Topic Hierarchy Best Practices

To optimize routing efficiency within SAP Advanced Event Mesh and avoid bloated subscription wildcards, we enforce a structured, predictable topic hierarchy layout:

$$\text{Domain} \,/\, \text{Business Unit} \,/\, \text{Geographic Zone} \,/\, \text{Substation ID} \,/\, \text{Asset Category} \,/\, \text{Asset ID} \,/\, \text{Event Type}$$

Examples:

  • grid/distribution/northeast/sub-s12/transformer/tx03/telemetry
  • grid/transmission/midwest/sub-s45/circuit-breaker/cb07/state

This allows BTP consumers to subscribe with surgical precision. For instance, a predictive maintenance engine can listen to all transformer telemetry across the entire Northeast region using the pattern: grid/distribution/northeast/+/transformer/+/telemetry.

7.      The Blueprint in Action: A Practitioner Scenario

To understand how this architecture operates under pressure, let's trace a real-world grid event: A severe thermal anomaly on Substation Transformer TX03 located in the Northeast distribution zone.

Step 1: The Edge Trigger (Purdue Level 2)

  1. The physical temperature sensor on Transformer TX03 detects a sudden spike, hitting $98.4^\circ\text{C}$.
  2. The local Intelligent Electronic Device (IED) broadcasts this telemetry via an IEC 61850 MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification) reporting block over the substation's internal fiber optic bus.

Step 2: Protocol Translation & Hardening (Industrial DMZ Level 3.5)

  1. The OT Industrial Edge Gateway (running Litmus Edge) intercepts the raw MMS packet.
  2. It strips out the heavy industrial headers, extracts the data fields from the Logical Node YPTR1 (the standard IEC 61850 node for a power transformer), and maps them into a standardized, web-friendly JSON payload.
  3. The gateway packages this payload inside a standardized CloudEvents v1.0 wrapper.

Step 3: Secure Egress to the DMZ Proxy (Enterprise DMZ Level 3.5)

  1. The Edge Gateway initiates an outbound-only TLS 1.3 connection to the Upper DMZ MQTT Broker (HiveMQ Cluster).
  2. It passes hardware-secured mTLS client certificates to prove its identity and publishes the message to the strict, isolated topic:

 “grid/distribution/northeast/sub-s12/transformer/tx03/telemetry”

Step 4: Ingestion by SAP Advanced Event Mesh (SAP BTP Layer)

  1. The HiveMQ cluster securely routes the incoming stream over a secure WebSocket connection into SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) via Dynamic Message Routing (DMR).
  2. Because AEM natively understands the hierarchical topic layout, it instantly clones and routes the message to three concurrent, decoupled subscribers without any database lag:
    • SAP Datasphere Ingestion Queue: For long-term historical time-series storage and grid analytics.
    • SAP AI Core Engine: To recalculate the transformer's Remaining Useful Life (RUL) score.
    • CAP Event Processor Queue: Dedicated to immediate, critical rule evaluation.

Step 5: Real-Time Orchestration & Clean Core Action

  1. A lightweight SAP CAP (Cloud Application Model) microservice consumes the message from its dedicated AEM queue.
  2. The service executes an automated rule: If temperature_celsius > $95^\circ\text{C}$ and cooling_fan_status == "ON_MAX", trigger emergency field intervention.
  3. Rather than writing complex custom ABAP tables inside the ERP, the CAP service uses the standard SAP Integration Suite to call the out-of-the-box S/4HANA OData v4 API (API_EQUIPMENT_SRV).
  4. The Result: An automated Urgent Corrective Maintenance Work Order is instantly generated in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management with a high-priority dispatch flag—all accomplished within 2.3 seconds of the physical temperature spike, leaving the digital core clean, secure, and upgrade-ready.

8.      Advanced Substation-to-BTP Integration Patterns

 *Reality Check*: Batch ingestion architectures are fundamentally incompatible with modern grid telemetry. Millisecond-level data streams require real-time, event-driven processing models—not delayed batch pipelines.

Depending on your edge compute availability and governance requirements, choose one of these production deployment topologies:

Pattern A: Multi-Tiered Message Broker Architecture (Recommended for Zero-Trust)

  • Mechanism: An Edge Gateway pushes local data to an on-premises DMZ MQTT cluster (e.g., Hive MQ). Secure bridge configuration clones and forwards permitted topics out to SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) via cloud connectors or secure WebSocket’s.
  • Pros: Strict isolation; if the cloud connection drops, the local DMZ broker caches events, mitigating data loss.

Pattern B: Intelligent Edge Compute with Stream Processing (Recommended for High Frequency)

  • Mechanism: Deploy an industrial edge compute node (running lightweight runtimes like Node-RED or KubeEdge) physically inside the substation. It aggregates millisecond telemetry, calculates rolling 1-minute means/standard deviations, and publishes only summarized statistical health events to SAP BTP.
  • Pros: Radically reduces egress data costs and avoids overwhelming BTP with trivial data points.

9.      Security & Governance Blueprint (Non-Negotiable Controls)

Bringing OT data to the cloud is impossible without an airtight security architecture. Implement the following parameters at each layer:

  • Mutual TLS (mTLS) with Hardware Roots of Trust: Every edge gateway must authenticate against the DMZ broker using X.509 client certificates issued by an internal Utility Certificate Authority (CA). Private keys must be stored in secure TPM 2.0 hardware chips on the edge device.
  • Granular Access Control Lists (ACLs): Edge gateways must be explicitly barred from publishing to topics outside their assigned asset scope. A compromise of Substation A must never allow injection of malicious events into Substation B.
  • Schema Validation at the Ingress Gate: Use the Schema Validation features in SAP Advanced Event Mesh. If a message arrives containing bad structures, malformed types, or executable code payloads, it is immediately shunted to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) for forensics, blocking code injection vectors.
  • Network Partitioning & Backpressure Controls: If the connection between the utility DMZ and SAP BTP degrades, the DMZ broker must utilize Local Storage Buffering. High QoS (Quality of Service) 1 or 2 settings must be applied strictly to critical operational state changes (e.g., breaker trips) to guarantee delivery, while standard continuous metrics use QoS 0 to save bandwidth.

10. Realizing Enterprise Value in SAP BTP

Once normalized grid events stream cleanly into SAP Advanced Event Mesh, they become actionable assets without touching the digital core directly:

*Strategic Insight*: Event Mesh is not optional—it is the control plane for grid-scale ingestion.  Without event-driven decoupling, high-frequency OT telemetry cannot be safely or scalable integrated into enterprise systems.

The Event Consumer Microservices Layer

Deploy lightweight, stateless microservices within the SAP BTP Kyma Runtime or SAP Cloud Application Model (CAP) to process incoming streams:

 

 

 

  • Real-Time Grid Insights: Kyma functions scan the event properties. If a transformer temperature exceeds defined thresholds (temperature_celsius > 95), an immediate event is triggered.
  • AI-Driven Asset Health Optimization: Data streams scale out directly into SAP Datasphere and SAP AI Core. Machine Learning models calculate remaining useful life (RUL) metrics based on historical load/thermal characteristics, sending updated health indices back to operational dashboards.

The Clean Core Imperative

By processing events asynchronously in BTP, the core enterprise system (SAP S/4HANA / IS-U) remains entirely untouched by heavy custom code. S/4HANA only participates when action is required—such as creating an asset maintenance order or executing a field service dispatch via standard standard OData v4 APIs. The system updates and upgrades effortlessly, unencumbered by massive custom databases full of RAW time-series data.

11. End-to-End Flow: Transformer Overheating Scenario

  • A transformer in Substation S12 exceeds thermal threshold (98.4°C).
  • IEC 61850 MMS reports are captured by Edge Gateway.
  • Gateway converts payload to Cloud Events JSON.
  • Event is securely published (mTLS) to DMZ MQTT broker.
  • Broker forwards allowed topics to SAP Advanced Event Mesh.
  • AEM routes the event to a CAP microservice.
  • CAP evaluates rules → triggers anomaly event.
  • SAP AI Core enriches with predictive health score.
  • SAP S/4HANA automatically creates a maintenance work order.
  • Field service team is dispatched proactively.

12. Operational Summary Blueprint

  1. The convergence of OT and IT is no longer optional—it is foundational to modern utilities.
  2. However, success depends not on connectivity alone, but on *how* that connectivity is governed. 
  3. Architectures that prioritize strict isolation, event-driven decoupling, and Clean Core principles will define the next generation of grid intelligence.
  4. SAP BTP, when used correctly, becomes not just an integration platform—but the control tower for real-time, autonomous utility operations.
